Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I'd try to block out time on weekends, maybe Sunday afternoons, but it rarely stuck. Most of my studying happened in bursts when I realized I was falling behind. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time creating diagrams and charts from the material, which took longer than just reading. With 16 hours a week when I could manage it, I tried to make it count, but consistency wasn't my strong suit.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what else was going on. Some weeks I'd be on top of things, other weeks I'd barely touch the course materials until a deadline loomed. I'm 24, so I've got responsibilities outside of school that younger students might not have. When work or personal stuff got intense, studying took a backseat. Deadlines were really what dictated when I'd buckle down and put in serious hours.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or knowing I had an assignment due soon. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about new content and that would prompt me to check in. I wasn't someone who logged in daily just to browse around. It was more functional for me—get in, find what I need, get out. Occasionally I'd log in because I felt guilty about not checking in for a while.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a habit for me the way social media or checking email is. There were maybe a couple weeks mid-semester where I was logging in more regularly because I was trying to catch up on videos. But that routine fell apart pretty quickly when other things demanded my attention. I wish it had become more automatic, but it just didn't click that way for me.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me by far. I'm a visual learner, so watching someone explain concepts while showing diagrams really helped things stick. I watched over four hours of video content because that format just works for my brain. The discussion boards were occasionally helpful when other students asked questions I hadn't thought of. Anything with visual elements—charts, infographics, video demonstrations—those were gold for me.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. Text-heavy resources just don't work well for how I learn, so I'd skim them at best. Some of the discussion prompts felt like busywork, so I only posted when I absolutely had to. I also delayed a lot of the practice quizzes because they felt less urgent than graded assignments. Looking back, I probably should have engaged with more of them.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to either understanding the material better or improving my grade. Visual content that broke down complex ideas was always worth it. If something felt like it was just checking a box without teaching me anything new, I'd deprioritize it. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers and get different perspectives on the material.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities always felt like they were on the back burner. Required stuff had deadlines and consequences, so those got my attention first. Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford with everything else going on. Even when I knew they might help, the lack of urgency meant they kept getting pushed down my list. It's not that I didn't see their value—I just couldn't justify the time when required work was piling up.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the lecture slides and videos early on so I could study offline. I'd print out diagrams and annotate them by hand, which helped me remember things better. I also used YouTube to find alternative explanations when the course material wasn't clicking. My notes were extensive—color-coded and full of my own drawings. A lot of my real learning happened with those physical materials spread out on my desk.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was studying hard but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd download a video once and watch it three or four times offline while taking notes. I spent hours redrawing diagrams from memory to test myself. Sometimes I'd study with a classmate in person, working through problems together without either of us being logged in. My exam score of 66 came from a lot of work that never touched the platform.
Q11: All the time. I'd download lecture videos and slides in one session, then spend days working through them without logging back in. My internet is reliable, but I just preferred having materials locally so I could study anywhere. I'd watch videos on my tablet during commutes or waiting around. That single download would fuel maybe a week's worth of studying that the system would never capture.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. My stress levels were pretty high, and I was dealing with some moderate anxiety about whether I could actually pass. There was a point where I felt so behind that logging into the LMS just made me feel worse about myself. The CBT sessions I did helped me reframe some of that thinking, which is probably why my mood improved later. But yeah, there were definitely weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to engage.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed, the LMS was the last place I wanted to be. Seeing all the incomplete tasks and missed deadlines just amplified my anxiety. Competing responsibilities—work, family stuff—meant the course sometimes had to take a backseat. When I was mentally drained, I'd maybe log in and stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Fatigue made it hard to engage meaningfully even when I tried.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be logged in, clicking through videos or readings, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd realize I'd watched ten minutes of a lecture without processing a single word. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Those sessions felt productive in the moment but looking back, I wasn't actually learning anything. It was engagement theater, basically.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was anxiety—starting felt overwhelming, so I'd avoid it. Part of it was that other things felt more urgent in the moment. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week. The CBT sessions actually helped me recognize this pattern and work on it, but I still struggled with avoidance throughout the course.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to engage. Most of my LMS activity probably clustered in the days before things were due. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I operated. Without a deadline, I could convince myself that other things were more important. The structure of due dates was the main thing keeping me accountable.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got positive feedback or saw I'd done better than expected, it motivated me to keep going. But negative feedback sometimes made me want to disengage because it felt discouraging. I tried to use feedback constructively, but with my anxiety, it was easy to spiral into negative thinking. The CBT sessions helped me process feedback more objectively instead of taking it personally.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work around my schedule, bad because I needed more external structure to stay on track. Having ten online courses meant a lot of self-direction was required, and I wasn't always great at providing that for myself. More regular check-ins or structured milestones might have helped me stay engaged more consistently.
Q19: The video content being available on-demand made engagement easier because I could learn in my preferred visual format whenever I had time. But the sheer volume of content was overwhelming sometimes—it was hard to know what to prioritize. The discussion requirements felt forced and didn't always add value. Clearer guidance on what was essential versus supplementary would have helped me focus my limited energy better.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure out the material but struggling to produce quality work. They might be watching videos repeatedly, reading discussions, searching for understanding, but still not confident enough to submit assignments. Or maybe they're dealing with perfectionism or anxiety that makes starting work feel impossible even though they're technically engaged. Activity doesn't always translate to output.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. My attendance and LMS engagement were low, but I still passed with decent exam scores. I did a lot of learning offline—downloaded materials, handwritten notes, self-testing with diagrams. The LMS numbers don't capture someone studying from printed slides on their couch or rewatching downloaded videos. Performance can come from effort that happens entirely outside the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on pages. That's useful for identifying who's completely disengaged. But they miss the quality of engagement and all the offline work. They can't tell if someone's actually learning or just clicking through. They miss the mental and emotional state of the student, the struggles that don't show up as data points.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, connecting ideas to things I already know. It's not about time spent or clicks made—it's about mental investment. For me, engagement looks like drawing diagrams, explaining concepts to myself, feeling curious about what comes next. It's internal, not something a system can easily measure.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety and how much energy it took just to open the platform some days. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed materials or the progress I made through CBT sessions that helped me actually function. It can't capture the resilience it took to keep going when I felt like giving up. My journey this semester was about more than logins and completion rates—it was about fighting through mental barriers to still pass the course.
